verdicts.title: Path Traversal via URI in Cache File readfile() (advanced-cache.php)
verdicts.description: In advanced-cache.php, the $cache_path variable is constructed by appending $_SERVER['REQUEST_URI'] (after urldecode and dot-stripping) directly to the cache directory path. The dot-stripping regex `preg_replace('/\.{2,}/', '', $uri)` removes sequences of two or more consecutive dots but does NOT prevent single-dot segments like `/.//`, URL-encoded sequences that survive urldecode and re-encode, or Unicode normalization bypasses. The path is then used in readfile() at line 173. However, the file_exists() check at line 153 acts as a guard: the attacker can only read files that already exist at the constructed path AND that the web process can read. Since the cache directory is under WP_CONTENT_DIR/cache/speedycache/, practical exploitation is limited to reading other cached pages or .gz files in the cache tree. The lack of realpath() or wp_normalize_path() before the existence check leaves a residual risk. A bypass using `/%2e%2e/` or `/%252e%252e/` after URL decoding could potentially be tested: urldecode converts %2e%2e to .., which are then stripped, but a double-encoded `%252e%252e` survives urldecode as `%2e%2e` and is not two raw dots, so the regex does not strip it. However PHP's file system operations would then see the literal string `%2e%2e` not as a path separator. Realistically the traversal is constrained to the cache dir contents.
verdicts.proof_of_concept: curl -H 'User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0' 'https://target.example.com/%2e%2e%2f%2e%2e%2f%2e%2e%2fetc%2fpasswd' -- In practice the readfile path will resolve under the cache dir so direct server-file reads are very unlikely without additional bypass. Testing should confirm whether the dot-stripping can be bypassed to reach sibling cache files belonging to other virtual hosts when WP_CONTENT_DIR is shared.
verdicts.remediation: Apply realpath() to the constructed $cache_path and verify the resolved path still begins with the expected cache directory prefix before calling readfile() or file_exists(). Example: $real = realpath($cache_path); if($real === false || strpos($real, realpath(WP_CONTENT_DIR.'/cache/speedycache/')) !== 0){ return; }

verdicts.title: Config File LFI via HTTP_HOST Injection in advanced-cache.php
verdicts.description: The static analysis flagged the include_once $config_file at line 84 as a Local File Inclusion risk. The $config_file path is built as: WP_CONTENT_DIR . '/speedycache-config/' . basename($_SERVER['HTTP_HOST']) . '.php'. The use of basename() on HTTP_HOST is a meaningful defense: basename() strips directory separators, meaning values like `../../etc/passwd` become just `passwd`. An attacker cannot traverse outside the speedycache-config directory through HTTP_HOST manipulation because basename() removes all path components except the last element. Additionally, the file must have a .php extension and already exist in the speedycache-config directory. No config file would exist for an attacker-supplied hostname, so the include branch is not reachable. The second form at line 73 (basename($_SERVER['HTTP_HOST']) . '.'. $site_dir . '.php') is also protected by basename(). No exploitable LFI exists here.
verdicts.proof_of_concept: N/A - basename() on HTTP_HOST neutralizes traversal. Attack vector does not exist.
verdicts.remediation: No fix required for LFI. However, consider also sanitizing $site_dir (derived from the URI path) before using it in the second config filename construction at line 73, as it is only dot-stripped but not basename-protected.

verdicts.title: Incomplete site_dir Sanitization in Config Filename Construction
verdicts.description: At advanced-cache.php line 63-73, $site_dir is derived from the first path segment of the URL: $parsed_path = explode('/', $path); $site_dir = $parsed_path[0]. The $path itself was already dot-stripped, so `..` becomes empty. However $site_dir is used unprotected in the second config file path: WP_CONTENT_DIR . '/speedycache-config/' . basename($_SERVER['HTTP_HOST']) . '.'. $site_dir . '.php'. While the preceding dots-stripping prevents classic ../ traversal, there are edge-case characters (null bytes in some PHP versions, or path segments like `.` single dot) that could confuse path resolution. Since only the second filename format is attempted when the first (host-only) config file does not exist, and the resulting path must still exist on disk, the exploitability is very low. Rated low confidence because the dot-stripping likely handles the most realistic bypasses and the file-existence check is a hard gate.
verdicts.proof_of_concept: curl -H 'Host: victim.com' 'https://target.example.com/%00evil' -- PHP null-byte injection in older versions. Modern PHP 5.3.4+ is immune to null-byte injection in file functions.
verdicts.remediation: Apply basename() or a strict alphanumeric+hyphen filter to $site_dir before using it in the config file path.

verdicts.title: Admin-Only Code Injection via var_export Config File Write
verdicts.description: In util.php set_config_file() (lines 147-175), the exclude rules stored in the speedycache_exclude option are retrieved from the database and passed directly to var_export(), then embedded in a PHP config file via file_put_contents(). var_export() does not escape PHP code - it produces syntactically valid PHP representations of the data, but if an array value itself contains a PHP closing tag or comment sequence followed by PHP code, the resulting output could be injected. For example, an exclude rule content value of: '); system('id'); //  would, after var_export, produce PHP code that executes the injected system() call when the config file is later include_once'd. The exclude rule 'content' is saved via Util::sanitize_request() which calls sanitize_text_field(). sanitize_text_field() does strip tags and extra whitespace but DOES NOT prevent the injection of PHP-breaking characters like single quotes, parentheses, or dollar signs. A concrete attack value that escapes the PHP array literal: `'); phpinfo(); //` saved as an exclude content. This requires admin access to call save_excludes(), but it's a privilege escalation from admin to RCE (which matters in multisite or with admin-level users who should not have code execution). Confidence is 'possible' because sanitize_text_field behavior with the specific character sequences needs empirical verification.
verdicts.proof_of_concept: 1. Log in as administrator. 2. POST to wp-admin/admin-ajax.php: action=speedycache_save_excludes&security=[valid nonce]&type=page&prefix=contains&content='); system('id'); // 3. The value gets saved to speedycache_exclude option and then written to WP_CONTENT_DIR/speedycache-config/example.com.php via var_export(). 4. The next page load includes this config file, executing the injected PHP.
verdicts.remediation: Do not use var_export() to serialize data into executable PHP files. Instead serialize the configuration as JSON: json_encode($export_config). In the config-template.php replace the PHP eval pattern with json_decode. Alternatively, use addslashes() on all string values before var_export, or validate that no string values contain single-quote characters.

verdicts.title: Missing Capability Check on delete_page_cache AJAX Handler
verdicts.description: The delete_page_cache() AJAX handler (ajax.php lines 47-56) calls check_ajax_referer() to verify the nonce but does NOT call current_user_can() to verify the user has any specific capability. Every other AJAX handler in the class checks current_user_can('manage_options'). Any authenticated user (subscriber role or above) who possesses a valid speedycache_ajax_nonce can call this endpoint. While the function body is incomplete (it only validates page_id, and the actual cache deletion logic appears to be missing), the missing authorization check is a confirmed defect. If the body is completed in a future version without adding the capability check, this becomes a privilege escalation allowing any subscriber to delete cache pages. Additionally, the nonce 'speedycache_ajax_nonce' is embedded in the admin page - if a subscriber can view any admin page that outputs this nonce, or if it leaks via other means, they can trigger this endpoint.
verdicts.proof_of_concept: 1. Log in as a subscriber. 2. Obtain speedycache_ajax_nonce value (may be present in admin-bar or any page the subscriber can view). 3. POST to wp-admin/admin-ajax.php: action=speedycache_delete_page_cache&_ajax_nonce=[nonce]&page_id=1. 4. The handler proceeds past the nonce check without any role verification.
verdicts.remediation: Add if(!current_user_can('manage_options')){ wp_send_json_error(__('Permission denied.', 'speedycache')); } immediately after check_ajax_referer() on line 48, consistent with all other handlers in the class.

verdicts.title: Settings Import File Upload - Adequate Security Controls
verdicts.description: The import_settings() handler (ajax.php lines 585-653) was flagged for use of $_FILES['file']['tmp_name'] with file_get_contents(). Review shows adequate security controls: (1) check_ajax_referer() verifies nonce; (2) current_user_can('manage_options') enforces admin-only access; (3) is_uploaded_file() checks the file is a genuine upload; (4) file extension is validated with regex to require .json; (5) filename must match the pattern speedycache-settings-YYYY-MM-DD*.json; (6) the file contents are JSON-decoded and only specific whitelisted option keys are updated via validate_and_sanitize_import_data(). The data is stored via update_option() which goes to the database, not directly executed. This is a false positive - the implementation is reasonably secure for an admin-only operation.
verdicts.remediation: No remediation required. The implementation correctly validates upload, restricts to admin, and sanitizes imported data. Consider adding MIME type checking (mime_content_type()) in addition to the filename extension check for defense in depth.

verdicts.title: No Nopriv AJAX Handlers - Cache Poisoning Not Applicable
verdicts.description: Static analysis raised cache poisoning concerns. Review of all AJAX hook registrations shows zero wp_ajax_nopriv_ hooks. Every AJAX endpoint requires authentication (wp_ajax_ prefix only). Cache clearing and manipulation functionality is restricted to admin users via current_user_can('manage_options'). Unauthenticated users cannot trigger cache clearing or manipulation via AJAX. The advanced-cache.php serves cache files passively based on filesystem state - it does not accept input that would modify what gets cached. This finding is a false positive.
verdicts.proof_of_concept: N/A - no nopriv AJAX handlers exist.

verdicts.title: Test Cache Path Overwrite via GET Parameter
verdicts.description: In advanced-cache.php lines 115-116, when the GET parameter test_speedycache is present, the cache_path is overridden to '/test' + $uri (where $uri is derived from the user-controlled REQUEST_URI after urldecode and dot-stripping). This means: $cache_path = '/test' + user_controlled_path. The subsequent readfile() call at line 173 would then attempt to read a file from an absolute path /test/<uri>/index.html. On most systems, /test/ either does not exist or is not web-accessible. However, this behavior is unintended for production: any unauthenticated visitor can supply ?test_speedycache=1 to trigger this code path and attempt file reads from /test/. The dot-stripping applies here too, so path traversal via dots is mitigated, but any readable file at /test/[uri]/index.html or /test/[uri]/index.html.gz on the server could be served. This is a low severity information disclosure risk, mainly relevant in configurations where /test/ happens to contain sensitive files readable by the web process.
verdicts.proof_of_concept: curl -H 'User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0' 'https://target.example.com/?test_speedycache=1' -- This triggers a readfile() from /test/<current_uri>/index.html instead of the normal cache path.
verdicts.remediation: Remove the test_speedycache special-case from production code, or gate it behind an admin check (e.g., require an additional secret token or verify a transient set only by an admin action). Also apply the same strict path normalization (realpath + prefix check) before any readfile() call.

summary: SpeedyCache (priority score 59) presents a mixed security picture. The most significant confirmed finding is a missing capability check on the delete_page_cache AJAX handler (VULN-005): any authenticated user can invoke it without admin rights, though the handler body is currently incomplete. The most impactful potential vulnerability is PHP code injection via var_export config file write (VULN-004): an admin who saves exclude rules with specially crafted content strings could inject PHP into the config file, which is then included on every page load - this represents an admin-to-RCE privilege escalation that matters in multisite environments. The originally flagged LFI via HTTP_HOST is a false positive due to basename() protection. The path traversal to readfile() is a low-to-medium risk constrained by file_exists() gating and the cache directory scope. No unauthenticated attack surface via AJAX exists (no nopriv handlers). The test_speedycache parameter creates a minor information disclosure risk. Overall the plugin's security posture is adequate for most settings operations but requires fixes to the missing authorization check and the var_export code generation pattern. Priority fixes: (1) add current_user_can check to delete_page_cache, (2) replace var_export config generation with JSON serialization, (3) add realpath() guard before readfile(), (4) restrict or remove the test_speedycache backdoor path.
